subUnits.title: 7.1 Estimating Dimensions & Distances
notes: Estimation means finding an approximate value close to the actual measurement without exact tools.
Use known references: average step ≈ 0.75m, arm span ≈ height, doorway ≈ 2m.
Round numbers to nearest 10, 100, or 1000 before calculating for quick mental math.
Example: Estimate classroom length by counting steps × 0.75. Check with a tape measure later.

quiz.explanation: Estimation gives a close, practical value when exact tools aren't available.

quiz.q: Best way to estimate corridor length?
opts: Count steps & multiply by average step length
Use a ruler for every meter
quiz.explanation: Using a consistent reference (step length) gives a reliable estimate.

subUnits.title: 7.2 Estimating Objects & Quantities
notes: Estimate mass, capacity, or number of items by grouping or comparing to known standards.
Examples: 1 bag of rice ≈ 50kg, 1 milk tin ≈ 1L, 1 textbook ≈ 0.5kg.
Rounding helps check if exact calculations are reasonable (e.g., if answer seems too high/low).
Used in markets, construction, inventory, and daily planning to save time and avoid errors.

quiz.q: Estimate mass of a standard JSS textbook.
A box holds 24 bottles. Estimate 9 boxes.

quiz.explanation: Estimation acts as a quick sanity check and speeds up decision-making.
